title: Add prediction mode for DH key exchange ('calculate Alice's public key before clicking Next')
description: Bloom's Level 3 (Apply). Before DH step 4, show prompt: 'Alice will compute A = g^a mod p. You know g=5, a=4, p=23. What is A?' Input field for numeric answer. On submit: green check if correct (with 'The modular arithmetic makes this one-way — easy to compute forward, hard to reverse'), red X if wrong (show the calculation step-by-step). This is the single highest-impact practice feature because DH has concrete math students can compute by hand. Source: Testing Effect (Roediger & Karpicke 2006) — retrieval practice retains 80% vs 34% passive.
acceptanceCriteria: Prediction prompt appears before DH steps 4, 5, 7 (public key calculations, shared secret)
Student enters numeric answer before seeing the result
Correct answer shows congratulatory message with reinforcing insight
Wrong answer shows step-by-step calculation and explains where they went wrong
Prediction can be skipped (optional, not blocking)

title: Add prediction mode for CORS ('will this configuration pass or fail?')
description: Bloom's Level 4 (Analyze). Before showing the CORS decision flow result, prompt: 'Given origin=app.example.com, target=api.example.com, method=PUT, headers=[Authorization] — will this request pass or fail? Why?' Multiple choice: (a) Pass — server allows this origin (b) Fail — PUT requires preflight and server doesn't allow it (c) Fail — Authorization header not in allowed headers. Show the decision flow AFTER the student answers, highlighting where their prediction was right or wrong.
acceptanceCriteria: After changing any CORS config parameter, a 'Predict the outcome' prompt appears
3-option multiple choice with specific reasons
After answering, decision flow highlights the step where the student's logic was correct/incorrect
Correct predictions tracked for scoring

title: Add 'You Are the CORS Browser' manual tracing mode
description: Bloom's Level 3-4 (Apply + Analyze). The student becomes the browser's CORS engine. Given a request config, they must click through decision nodes: 'Is this same-origin? [Yes/No]' → 'Is this a simple request? [Yes/No]' → 'Send preflight? [Yes/No]' → 'Server allows origin? [Yes/No]'. Each wrong click shows WHY it's wrong using the existing CORS decision flow visualization. This transforms passive viewing into active decision-making. Source: Brilliant.org pattern of interactive problem-solving.
acceptanceCriteria: Toggle button switches between 'Watch' mode (existing) and 'Trace' mode
In Trace mode, each decision point requires student click before proceeding
Wrong clicks show immediate feedback with the correct reasoning
At least 5 different CORS scenarios pre-configured for tracing practice

title: Add 'You Are the Attacker (XSS)' interactive challenge mode
description: Bloom's Level 5 (Evaluate) + Level 6 (Create). Show a simulated vulnerable web page with an input field. Student types XSS payload. System shows: (1) how the server processes the input (raw echo vs encoding), (2) whether the payload would execute, (3) what the attacker could steal. Progressive difficulty: Level 1 = reflected XSS with no defense, Level 2 = reflected with HTML encoding (student must find bypass), Level 3 = stored XSS scenario. Source: Hacksplaining 'hack then learn' pattern + picoCTF progressive difficulty.
acceptanceCriteria: Input field where student types XSS payload
Visual simulation shows payload flowing through server and being rendered
Successful payload triggers visual 'attack succeeded' with explanation of impact
Failed payload explains WHY the defense blocked it
At least 3 difficulty levels with hints available

title: Add 'You Are the JWT Validator' step-by-step verification challenge
description: Bloom's Level 3-4 (Apply + Analyze). Given a JWT token string, student must: (1) identify the 3 parts (click where the dots are), (2) decode the header (select base64url decode), (3) check the algorithm (select 'is HS256 safe?'), (4) verify the signature (compute HMAC and compare), (5) check expiration (compare exp with current time). Each wrong step shows what would happen in a real attack (e.g., skipping signature check = accepting forged tokens).
acceptanceCriteria: Challenge mode toggle in JWT Lifecycle view
Student must click through 5 validation steps in order
Wrong step order shows security implication
Token includes deliberate traps (expired, wrong algorithm) that student must catch
At least 3 pre-built challenge tokens with different vulnerabilities

title: Add 'Spot the CORS Bug' debugging challenge
description: Bloom's Level 5 (Evaluate). Show a CORS server configuration with a subtle bug. Student must identify it. Challenge 1: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` (incompatible). Challenge 2: Missing `Vary: Origin` header (cache poisoning). Challenge 3: Overly permissive `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` reflecting any origin (no whitelist). Show the vulnerability explanation when student identifies the bug.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 3 debugging challenges with different CORS bugs
Student clicks on the buggy line/header to identify it
Correct identification shows vulnerability explanation and fix
Wrong identification shows hint about what to look for

title: Add 'Spot the JWT Vulnerability' debugging challenge
description: Bloom's Level 5 (Evaluate). Show JWT validator pseudocode with vulnerabilities. Challenge 1: Validator reads algorithm from token header (alg confusion attack). Challenge 2: No expiration check (replay attack). Challenge 3: Secret is 'password123' (weak secret brute-force). Student identifies the bug and selects the attack it enables.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 3 JWT debugging challenges
Each shows pseudocode with a highlighted vulnerability
Student selects which attack this vulnerability enables
Correct answer links to the corresponding JWT Attacks visualization

title: Add scenario-based OAuth flow selection challenge (5 scenarios)
description: Bloom's Level 5 (Evaluate). Present real-world scenarios, student must choose correct OAuth flow. (1) 'React SPA calling your API' → Auth Code + PKCE, (2) 'Backend microservice calling payment API' → Client Credentials, (3) 'Smart TV Netflix login' → Device Authorization, (4) 'Mobile app with refresh token' → Auth Code + PKCE + Refresh, (5) 'Server-rendered app with client secret' → Auth Code (no PKCE needed). Wrong answers explain why the chosen flow doesn't fit.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 scenario cards with multiple-choice flow selection
Each scenario describes a real product/use case
Wrong answers explain specifically why that flow doesn't fit
Correct answers link to the corresponding OAuth flow visualization

title: Add scenario-based encryption/algorithm selection challenge (5 scenarios)
description: Bloom's Level 5 (Evaluate). (1) 'Encrypt 10GB backup' → AES (symmetric, fast), (2) 'Send credit card to payment API' → Hybrid TLS (RSA key exchange + AES data), (3) 'Sign a JWT' → HS256 (shared secret) or RS256 (if verifiers shouldn't sign), (4) 'Store passwords in database' → bcrypt (slow hash, not encryption), (5) 'Two servers need shared secret, no pre-shared key' → DH key exchange then AES.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 scenario cards with algorithm/technique selection
Each wrong answer explains why that choice is wrong or suboptimal
Correct answers link to relevant topic visualization

title: Add CSP policy builder challenge ('design a policy for this app')
description: Bloom's Level 6 (Create). Present a web app description: 'React app hosted on app.example.com, calls api.example.com, loads Google Fonts, embeds YouTube videos, uses inline styles (Tailwind).' Student must build a CSP policy by toggling directives. System validates: script-src must include 'self' but NOT 'unsafe-inline' (use nonce instead), font-src must include fonts.googleapis.com, frame-src must include youtube.com. Each wrong directive shows what attack it would allow.
acceptanceCriteria: Interactive CSP builder with directive toggles
At least 3 app scenarios with different requirements
Each directive toggle shows what it allows/blocks visually
Validation explains WHY each directive is needed or dangerous

title: Add SQLi payload crafting challenge with live query visualization
description: Bloom's Level 5-6 (Evaluate + Create). Show a login form backed by a SQL query template. Student types username/password. System shows: (1) the resulting SQL query with student's input interpolated, (2) whether the query returns rows (login success/fail), (3) which rows matched. Challenge: bypass login. Progressive: Level 1 = basic ' OR '1'='1, Level 2 = UNION-based extraction, Level 3 = blind SQLi. Then show parameterized query defense.
acceptanceCriteria: Login form with live SQL query preview
Student types input and sees resulting SQL query in real-time
Successful injection shows 'Login bypassed!' with rows returned
After solving, parameterized query version shows why injection fails
At least 3 difficulty levels

title: Add AES S-box lookup prediction challenge
description: Bloom's Level 3 (Apply). Before AES SubBytes step, prompt: 'The byte at [0,0] is 0x32. After S-box substitution, what will it become? (Look at the S-box table.)' Show the S-box table. Student enters hex value. System checks against SBOX[0x32]. This teaches students to READ the S-box and understand that it's a fixed lookup table, not a formula.
acceptanceCriteria: Prediction prompt before SubBytes step with S-box table reference
Student enters hex value
Correct answer highlights the S-box cell that was used
Wrong answer shows the correct lookup path in the S-box table

title: Add security concept flashcard system with spaced repetition
description: Bloom's Level 1 (Remember). Generate flashcards from each topic's key takeaways: 'Q: What prevents CORS preflight? A: Simple methods (GET/HEAD/POST) with safe-listed headers.' 'Q: What does the S in PKCE stand for? A: Proof Key for Code Exchange.' Use SM-2 spaced repetition algorithm (same as Anki). Track per-card ease factor. Show daily review count. Source: Ebbinghaus forgetting curve + Anki SM-2 algorithm.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 30 flashcards generated from existing 13 topics (2-3 per topic)
Flashcard review with 'Again / Hard / Good / Easy' buttons
SM-2 scheduling stored in localStorage
Daily review count shown in sidebar
Cards resurface based on spaced repetition intervals

title: Add practice scoring system with streaks, XP, and per-topic mastery
description: Gamification layer for all practice features. Points: 10 XP per correct prediction, 25 XP per completed challenge, 50 XP per debugging challenge solved. Streak: consecutive correct answers (multiplier: streak × base XP). Per-topic mastery: 0-5 stars based on prediction accuracy + challenges completed. Overall progress: 'X/13 topics mastered.' Weekly streak: number of days this week with at least 1 practice attempt. Source: Duolingo — streaks increase commitment by 60%.
acceptanceCriteria: XP counter visible in module header or sidebar
Streak counter (consecutive correct answers)
Per-topic mastery stars (0-5) in sidebar next to topic names
Progress persists in localStorage
Weekly activity indicator
